title: Critical vulnerability SLA tracker — unpatched 30+ days with exploited-in-the-wild indicator
exclusions: Does NOT validate compensating controls; that is a separate manual process.
evidence: Daily report 'critical_vuln_sla' writes per-host per-CVE SLA evidence to compliance_summary.

compliance.assurance_rationale: RA-5 vulnerability scanning — the UC produces SLA evidence for remediation timeliness.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIST 800-53 RA-5 (Vulnerability scanning) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.43.1: Critical vulnerability SLA tracker — unpatched 30+ days with exploited-in-the-wild indicator.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.43.1' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: PCI 6.3 requires addressing vulnerabilities within a defined window — 30 days for critical.

controlTest.positiveScenario: When a CVE with CVSS ≥ 9.0 or marked exploited-in-the-wild is observed on a host for more than 30 days without a remediation record, the UC fires.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Hosts remediated within 30 days or hosts with an accepted-risk token (valid period) do NOT fire.

dataSources: See subcategory preamble. — supplemented by authentication logs, system audit trails, and configuration management database (CMDB) asset inventory exports
spl:
index=vuln sourcetype=tenable:vm OR sourcetype=qualys:vm OR sourcetype=rapid7:vm earliest=-60d cvss_v3_base>=9.0
| stats min(_time) as first_seen max(_time) as last_seen values(cve) as cves by host
| eval age_days=round((now()-first_seen)/86400,0)
| where age_days>30
| join type=outer host cves [search index=vuln action=remediated]
| where isnull(remediated_at)
| table host cves first_seen age_days
description: Continuously measures how long critical or exploited-in-the-wild vulnerabilities have persisted. Breaches are surfaced immediately and feed the evidence pack with per-host details.
value: A missed critical patch is the #1 root cause of breaches — proving remediation timeliness is the matching audit control.
implementation: (1) Ingest scanner data into Splunk via TA; (2) consume CISA KEV list into exploited_in_wild.csv; (3) run daily; (4) integrate with SOAR to auto-open remediation. Indexes required: `index=vuln`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=qualys:vm`, `sourcetype=rapid7:vm`, `sourcetype=tenable:vm`.
visualization: Bar chart of age buckets, table of breaches, single-value '% of critical CVEs remediated within 30 days'. Key fields: `age_days`, `cves`, `first_seen`, `host`, `last_seen`.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK — T1190 Exploit Public-Facing Application

knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised security assessment activities** — Planned security control assessments, continuous monitoring scans, and Plan of Action and Milestones (POA&M) validation tests generate expected control deviation signals that are part of the Assessment & Authorisation process.

2. **System authorisation boundary changes** — During ATO re-authorisation, system boundary expansions, or inheritance model updates, temporary control gaps may appear in the monitoring baseline until the updated security plan is approved by the Authorising Official.

3. **Interconnection Security Agreement (ISA) activity** — Data exchanges with authorised external systems operating under signed ISAs produce cross-boundary traffic that triggers network monitoring alerts but is covered by the formal interconnection agreement.

4. **FIPS 199 impact-level re-categorisation** — When system categorisation changes (e.g., Moderate to High), the transition period produces control baseline mismatches until the enhanced controls are fully implemented per the approved implementation schedule.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `nist_800_53_exception_filter` KV Store lookup containing approved assessment schedule entries, POA&M IDs, ISA identifiers, and system re-categorisation project IDs. The correlation search filters against this lookup and suppresses matches with valid exception entries.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Technology Add-on**: Tenable Add-on for Splunk (Splunkbase app ID 4060) must be installed on search heads and indexers. Verify with `| rest /services/apps/local | search label="Tenable Add-on for Splunk" | table label version`.
• **Indexes**: `vuln` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Search-head capacity**: Subsearch-based detections need `subsearch_maxtime` ≥ 60s and `subsearch_maxout` ≥ 50000 in `limits.conf`. For high-volume environments, tune `[search] max_searches_per_cpu` up from default.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `RA-5`. The control objective is: "Evidence that NIST 800-53 RA-5 (Vulnerability scanning) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.43.1: Critical vulnerability SLA tracker — unpatched 30+ days with exploited-in-the-wild indicator." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is performed by the Tenable Add-on for Splunk (Splunkbase app ID 4060), landing events in `vuln` under `tenable:vm`, `qualys:vm`, `rapid7:vm`. Daily vulnerability deltas for a 10000-asset estate produce 50000-500000 findings/day (~5-50 GB/month). Full snapshots weekly add more.

Configure the input through Settings > Data Inputs > Tenable.sc Vulnerability. A reference `inputs.conf` stanza looks like:

```ini
[tenable_sc_vuln://prod]
endpoint = https://tenable.example.com
interval = 86400
index = vulnerability
sourcetype = tenable:sc:vuln
```

The following fields are authoritative for this detection once the TA parses them: `pluginID, severity, state, first_seen, last_seen, host, dns, ip, plugin_name, cve`. Verify they are extracted at search time with `index=vuln earliest=-15m | fieldsummary` before scheduling the detection.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Ingest scanner data into Splunk via TA. Ingestion SLA drift is usually invisible until it breaks something — monitor the feed's freshness with a companion search that alerts when the newest event is older than the expected cadence.

• **Hint 2:** consume CISA KEV list into exploited_in_wild.csv. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** run daily. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** integrate with SOAR to auto-open remediation. Automated remediation must be gated behind a defined approval workflow; compliance controls that take destructive action without human review are themselves a governance risk.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "Critical vulnerability SLA tracker — unpatched 30+ days with exploited-in-the-wild indicator," the aggregation produces the risk posture — the per-row values should reconcile to the organisation's risk register entries, not stand alone as engineering metrics.

This search correlates the outer dataset (index=`vuln`, sourcetype=`tenable:vm`) with a subsearch on `host cves` across the last 60 days. `type=outer` (left-outer) returns every row from the outer search and fills nulls where the inner search has no match. Use this when the compliance question is 'for every row in A, what does B say?' — the null pattern is itself the finding (e.g. 'ticket opened but no PIR recorded').

Join caveat: Splunk's `| join` subsearch is capped at `max_searches_per_cpu * subsearch_maxout` results (default: 50000). If the inner subsearch exceeds this, rows are silently dropped and the resulting report will under-count. Check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd log_level=WARN subsearch` for truncation warnings. For inner searches expected to return >10000 rows, rewrite as `| stats` with a larger base search and post-filter, or use `| tstats` if a CIM datamodel covers the data.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.43.1: Critical vulnerability SLA tracker — unpatched 30+ days with]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -60d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.43.1,reg=NIST_800-53
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=vuln sourcetype=tenable:vm earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `age_days`, `cves`, `first_seen`, `host`, `last_seen`. Run:

```spl
index=vuln sourcetype=tenable:vm earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table age_days, cves, first_seen, host, last_seen
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `cvss_v3_base`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(cvss_v3_base) as p50 perc90(cvss_v3_base) as p90 perc99(cvss_v3_base) as p99 max(cvss_v3_base) as hi
```

The current threshold `9.0` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.43.1: NIST 800-53 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Bar Chart Of Age Buckets (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Table Of Breaches (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Single-Value '% Of Critical Cves Remediated Within 30 Days' (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** The most likely cause is subsearch truncation (see Step 2). Inspect `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=searchprocessor log_level=WARN subsearch` for messages like 'Output result count exceeded 50000.'

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NIST 800-53-specific failure modes:**

• Control baseline selection: NIST 800-53 has Low/Moderate/High/Privacy baselines via FIPS 199 categorisation. A detection written to a High baseline threshold produces false alarms against Low-categorised systems. Join with the system categorisation register `nist_800_53_system_categorization.csv` before thresholding.

• CA-7 continuous monitoring cadence: specifies 'ongoing' authorisation activities. Detections supporting CA-7 must produce metrics at the organisation's ConMon cadence (monthly is common for most agencies) — weekly fires are fine but not themselves evidence of monthly compliance.

• Control inheritance: in a cloud-hybrid deployment, some controls are inherited from the Cloud Service Provider. Detections must not flag 'missing' controls the CSP provides (documented in the SSP via Control Inheritance matrix); reference the SSP appendix and suppress matching findings.

• SP 800-53 Rev 5 restructure: controls were renumbered between Rev 4 and Rev 5. Detections that cite Rev 4 numbering (e.g. AC-2.12) may not match the SSP if the organisation has migrated. Store both Rev numbers in the UC metadata and reconcile.
grandmaExplanation: We help you see patch timing against your service promises so you can explain delays with evidence.
